title: Identifying Effective Behavior Change Strategies For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •

164
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 22a
Identifying Effective Behavior Change Strategies
For each A and B pair, check the more effective response.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 1A.	When you are trying to increase a
behavior, it is most effective to wait
for the full desired behavior before
reinforcing, so the person does not
think that halfway is good enough.

 1B.	When you are trying to increase
a behavior, it is most effective to
reinforce small improvement in the
right direction, or else the person may
not continue to improve.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 5A.	If a person’s problem behaviors
work to get things he or she wants,
it is most effective to punish those
behaviors to make them stop.

 5B.	If a person’s problem behaviors work
to get things he or she wants, it is
most effective to stop reinforcing
those behaviors and instead give
rewards when the person uses more
skillful strategies to get what he or she
wants or needs.
••••••••••

 2A.	The most effective punishment
is intense anger and swift verbal
criticism.

 2B.	The most effective punishment is to
find one that fits the severity of the
problem behavior.
••••••••••••

 6A.	When you are punishing, figure that
a nonspecific punishment will be a
lot more effective, since it can’t be
avoided.

 6B.	Use a specific and time-limited
negative consequence to decrease
behavior.
••••••••••

 3A.	It is most effective to reinforce
behavior immediately after it occurs.

 3B.	It is most effective to reward behavior
after a delay so that the person
does not expect that you will always
provide a reward.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 7A.	If a person’s mean behavior makes
you feel hurt, it is most effective to
punish the behavior by taking away
gifts that you previously gave the
person.

 7B.	If a person’s mean behavior makes
you feel hurt, it is most effective to
punish the behavior by not doing
favors for the person until his or her
behavior improves.
••••••••••

 4A.	It is common that people reward
others’ problematic behaviors without
even realizing it.

 4B.	People do not reward others’
problematic behaviors, because that
would be stupid.
••••••••••••••

 8A.	After a punished behavior stops, it is
most effective to reward an alternative
behavior that you want.

 8B.	After a punished behavior stops,
it is most effective to continue the
punishment, so that you send a very
clear message that the problematic
behavior is unacceptable.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Identifying Effective Behavior Change Strategies For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
